methodology_sections: 1. Lifecycle Integrity - State transitions, illegal states, predecessors
2. Monotonicity & Irreversibility - Increasing values, one-way transitions
3. Access & Permission Logic - Conditional access, role requirements
4. Time & Resource Integrity - Time-locks, resource bounds, expiry

invariants.description: Bribe.withdraw silently fails when amount > balanceOf[tokenId], causing desynchronization between Voter and Bribe accounting. The Voter believes it withdrew votes, but the Bribe retains the balance, allowing permanent phantom bribe rewards.
invariants.attack_scenario: 1. User votes, depositing voting power X into bribe. 2. Voter recalculates weight at reset time to Y > X (e.g., due to veNFT balance changes). 3. Voter calls bribe.withdraw(Y, tokenId). 4. Y > balanceOf[tokenId] so withdraw silently does nothing. 5. Voter's internal state shows the vote is reset, but bribe still has balanceOf[tokenId] = X. 6. User continues earning bribe rewards indefinitely without active votes, draining bribe rewards from legitimate voters.
invariants.invariant_logic: For every withdraw(amount, tokenId) call from voter: assert(amount <= balanceOf[tokenId]) OR revert
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_withdraw_must_succeed() public { // After voter.reset(tokenId), assert(bribe.balanceOf(tokenId) == 0); }

invariants.description: calculate_rebase can revert due to arithmetic underflow when veBlackSupply exceeds blackSupply, permanently blocking all epoch emissions. The smNFTBalance and smNFT bonus are added to veBlackSupply but only the bonus is added to blackSupply, so if smNFTBalance is large enough, the subtraction underflows.
invariants.attack_scenario: 1. smNFTBalance grows large (many supermassive NFTs created). 2. veBlackSupply = _veTotal + _smNFTBalance + _superMassiveBonus. 3. blackSupply = _blackTotal + _superMassiveBonus. 4. If _veTotal + _smNFTBalance > _blackTotal, then circulatingBlack = blackSupply - veBlackSupply underflows. 5. Solidity 0.8 reverts on underflow, blocking update_period() permanently. 6. No emissions, no distributions, protocol is frozen.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_no_rebase_underflow() public view { uint veBlack = black.balanceOf(address(ve)) + ve.smNFTBalance() + ve.calculate_sm_nft_bonus(ve.smNFTBalance()); uint totalBlack = black.totalSupply() + ve.calculate_sm_nft_bonus(ve.smNFTBalance()); assert(totalBlack >= veBlack); }

invariants.description: setReferralFee has no upper bound check on dibsPercentage. Since REFERRAL_FEE_DENOMINATOR is 1000, setting dibsPercentage >= 1000 would drain 100% or more of LP fees, and values > 1000 cause underflow in GaugeCL._claimFees when subtracting dibs fees from claimed amounts.
invariants.attack_scenario: 1. GAUGE_ADMIN (or compromised owner) calls setReferralFee(1000). 2. In GaugeCL._claimFees, _dibsFeeToken0 = claimed0 * 1000 / 1000 = claimed0. 3. All fees go to dibs address, nothing to internal bribe. 4. If set to > 1000, _dibsFeeToken0 > claimed0, causing underflow revert that blocks all fee distribution.

invariants.description: When notifyRewardAmount is called with totalWeight == 0 (no votes cast), the transferred tokens are locked in GaugeManager permanently. The ratio becomes 0, index is not updated, and those tokens can never be distributed to any gauge since no recovery/sweep mechanism exists.
invariants.attack_scenario: 1. All veNFT holders reset votes before epoch flip. 2. Minter calls update_period which calls gaugeManager.notifyRewardAmount(_gauge). 3. IVoter(voter).totalWeight() returns 0. 4. _ratio = 0, index unchanged, but tokens already transferred to GaugeManager. 5. Tokens permanently stuck, representing lost epoch emissions.
invariants.invariant_logic: When notifyRewardAmount(amount) is called with amount > 0: IVoter(voter).totalWeight() > 0
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_notify_requires_weight() public { uint tw = IVoter(voter).totalWeight(); assert(tw > 0 || IERC20(base).balanceOf(address(gaugeManager)) == 0); }

invariants.description: setInternalBribe uses 'require(_int >= address(0))' which is always true for any address value (addresses are unsigned). This allows setting internal_bribe to address(0), which would cause _claimFees to revert when trying to call notifyRewardAmount on address(0), permanently blocking fee distribution for that gauge.
invariants.attack_scenario: 1. Owner calls setInternalBribe(address(0)). 2. Check passes since any address >= address(0). 3. internal_bribe = address(0). 4. When _claimFees is called, IERC20.safeApprove(address(0),...) or IBribe(address(0)).notifyRewardAmount() reverts. 5. Fee distribution permanently blocked for this gauge.
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_internal_bribe_nonzero() public view { assert(internal_bribe != address(0)); }

invariants.description: Gauge distribution timestamp must strictly increase per epoch. The gaugesDistributionTimestmap is only updated when claimable > 0 AND isAlive AND !emergency. If a gauge has 0 claimable (no votes) or is in emergency, _updateForAfterDistribution is called repeatedly without updating the timestamp, but this is safe because supplyIndex tracks correctly. However, the killed→revived transition does NOT reset supplyIndex, which is intentional — but if a gauge is revived after index has advanced significantly, its first distribution could be zero (supplyIndex catches up without accruing).
invariants.attack_scenario: 1. Gauge is killed at epoch E, supplyIndex = X. 2. Multiple epochs pass, global index advances to Y >> X. 3. During each _updateForAfterDistribution on killed gauge, delta (Y-X) worth of tokens are sent back to minter, and supplyIndex catches up. 4. Gauge is revived. 5. supplyIndex already equals current index, so no immediate backpay. This is correct behavior protecting against revival exploitation, but must be documented.
invariants.invariant_logic: For killed gauge: supplyIndex[gauge] is always updated to current index (no reward accumulation). For revived gauge: old(supplyIndex) == index at revival time
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_killed_gauge_no_accumulation() public view { for each gauge g: if (!isAlive[g]) assert(claimable[g] == 0); }

invariants.description: Bribe.earned contains an unbounded loop iterating over all unclaimed epochs. With WEEK = 1800 seconds (30 minutes), epochs accumulate 48 per day. If a user does not claim for extended periods, the loop in earned() will exceed block gas limit, permanently locking their bribe rewards.
invariants.attack_scenario: 1. User votes and deposits into bribe at epoch 0. 2. User does not claim for 30 days. 3. numEpochs = 30 * 48 = 1440 iterations needed. 4. Each iteration requires multiple SLOAD operations (checkpoint lookups). 5. Gas cost exceeds block limit, getReward reverts permanently for this tokenId. 6. User's bribe rewards are locked forever with no partial claim mechanism.
invariants.invariant_logic: numEpochs = (epochStart(block.timestamp) - lastEarnEpoch) / WEEK must be bounded by a maximum to prevent DoS
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_earned_bounded() public view { uint maxEpochs = 200; for each tokenId with lastEarn: assert((BlackTimeLibrary.epochStart(block.timestamp) - BlackTimeLibrary.epochStart(lastEarn[token][tokenId])) / WEEK <= maxEpochs); }

invariants.description: The quorum function uses getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() without a timestamp parameter, meaning it calculates quorum based on current smNFT total supply rather than historical supply at snapshot time. An attacker can manipulate quorum by creating/destroying smNFTs between proposal creation and vote execution.
invariants.attack_scenario: 1. Attacker submits a proposal. 2. Quorum is checked against current smNFT total supply. 3. Attacker creates additional smNFTs to increase total supply, raising the quorum threshold. 4. Legitimate proposal fails to meet the artificially inflated quorum. 5. Alternatively, attacker deflates smNFT supply to lower quorum for their own proposal.
invariants.invariant_logic: quorum(blockTimestamp) must use historical smNFT supply at the proposal's snapshot timestamp, not current supply
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_quorum_uses_snapshot() public view { // quorum should equal: (token.getsmNFTPastTotalSupply(proposalSnapshot) * quorumNumerator()) / quorumDenominator() }

invariants.description: The clock() and CLOCK_MODE() functions have empty implementations returning default values (0 and empty string). These are required by OpenZeppelin's Governor framework for correct proposal timing, snapshot calculation, and EIP-6372 compliance. Empty implementations will cause incorrect proposal lifecycle timing.
invariants.attack_scenario: 1. clock() returns 0 for all calls. 2. Proposal snapshots are calculated using clock(), yielding timestamp 0. 3. Voting power lookups use timestamp 0, returning 0 for all voters. 4. No proposals can ever reach quorum. 5. Alternatively, if governance framework falls back to block.number, proposals may use wrong time references, allowing vote manipulation.
invariants.invariant_logic: clock() must return current block.timestamp (or block.number per chosen mode); CLOCK_MODE() must return 'mode=timestamp' or 'mode=blocknumber'

invariants.description: The epochCount variable increments every update_period call but the emission phase transition at epochCount == 15 (growth to decay) is irreversible. However, if setGaugeManager is called to a new GaugeManager, the new manager's index starts at 0 while the minter continues with the same epochCount. More critically, the tailEmissionRate is reset to MAX_BPS every epoch in update_period, but nudge() can only be called once per period. If nudge is not called, emissions neither increase nor decrease (rate = 10000 = 100%). The nudge governance mechanism is the ONLY way to change tail emission rate, and it requires weekly governance proposals.
invariants.invariant_logic: epochCount strictly increases: old(epochCount) < epochCount after update_period; proposals[period] must be set before tailEmissionRate takes effect; tailEmissionRate is reset to MAX_BPS at start of each epoch
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_epoch_monotonic() public view { assert(minter.epochCount() >= 0); assert(minter.tailEmissionRate() >= MINIMUM_TAIL_RATE && minter.tailEmissionRate() <= MAXIMUM_TAIL_RATE || minter.tailEmissionRate() == MAX_BPS || minter.tailEmissionRate() == PROPOSAL_INCREASE || minter.tailEmissionRate() == PROPOSAL_DECREASE); }

invariants.description: EpochController.initialize does not set genesisManager, but performUpkeep calls genesisManager.checkAtEpochFlip() which will revert on address(0). If setGenesisManager is not called before the first epoch flip automation, the entire epoch upkeep process fails, blocking distributions.
invariants.attack_scenario: 1. EpochController is deployed and initialized without genesisManager. 2. Chainlink automation or EPOCH_MANAGER calls performUpkeep. 3. genesisManager is address(0), calling checkAtEpochFlip() on zero address reverts. 4. distributeAll() and distributeFees() are never reached. 5. Entire protocol emissions and fee distributions are blocked until genesisManager is set.
invariants.invariant_logic: Before performUpkeep can succeed: genesisManager != address(0)

invariants.description: _setExternalBribe emits the wrong old value in the event - it references internal_bribes[_gauge] instead of external_bribes[_gauge]. This is a copy-paste bug that corrupts event logs, making off-chain monitoring and governance tracking unreliable.
invariants.attack_scenario: 1. GAUGE_ADMIN calls setExternalBribeFor(gauge, newExtBribe). 2. Event SetBribeFor(false, internal_bribes[gauge], newExtBribe, gauge) is emitted. 3. Off-chain monitoring sees internal bribe address as the 'old external bribe'. 4. Governance dashboards and audit trails show incorrect bribe history. 5. If a security incident requires tracing bribe changes, the wrong addresses are logged.
invariants.invariant_logic: SetBribeFor event for external bribe must emit external_bribes[_gauge] as old value, not internal_bribes[_gauge]

invariants.description: claimRewards(address _gauge, uint256[] memory _nftIds, bool isBonusReward) has no validation that _gauge is a registered CL gauge (no isGauge or isCLGauge check). An attacker can pass any contract address as _gauge. While GaugeCL.getReward has onlyDistribution, a malicious contract could implement getReward without that restriction and execute arbitrary logic when called by GaugeManager.
invariants.attack_scenario: 1. Attacker deploys malicious contract implementing IGaugeCL interface. 2. Attacker calls gaugeManager.claimRewards(maliciousContract, nftIds, false). 3. GaugeManager calls maliciousContract.getReward() without checking isGauge. 4. Malicious contract executes callback into GaugeManager or other protocol contracts. 5. Since claimRewards lacks nonReentrant, state manipulation via reentrancy is possible.

invariants.description: The _initialize function can only be called once (by _initializer), but it sets active_period to current week, enabling immediate minting. If _initialize is frontrun or called with malicious parameters (claimants/amounts), initial veNFT distribution is corrupted. Additionally, if _initializer never calls _initialize, update_period is permanently blocked since it requires _initializer == address(0).
invariants.attack_scenario: 1. MinterUpgradeable is deployed and initialized via initialize(). 2. _initializer is set to deployer. 3. If deployer key is compromised before _initialize is called, attacker calls _initialize with their own claimants, stealing initial token distribution. 4. _initializer is set to address(0), locking out any correction. 5. Alternatively, if _initialize is never called, _initializer remains non-zero, and update_period always skips (condition: _initializer == address(0) fails).
invariants.invariant_logic: _initialize must be called exactly once; after call: _initializer == address(0) && active_period == (block.timestamp / WEEK) * WEEK
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_initializer_consumed() public view { assert(minter._initializer() == address(0)); }

invariants.description: distributeFees(uint256 _start, uint256 _finish) does not validate that _finish <= pools.length, allowing an out-of-bounds array access that reverts the entire transaction. Similarly, distribute(uint256 _start, uint256 _finish) has no bounds check.
invariants.attack_scenario: 1. pools.length = 10. 2. Caller invokes distributeFees(0, 20). 3. When x = 10, pools[10] causes out-of-bounds revert. 4. Entire transaction reverts, no fees are distributed. 5. If called by automation, automation repeatedly fails until parameters are corrected.
invariants.invariant_logic: _finish <= pools.length for all ranged distribution calls
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_distribute_bounds(uint256 _start, uint256 _finish) public view { assert(_finish <= pools.length && _start <= _finish); }
